coredata.dc:title: Blue/yellow emissive carbon dots coupled with curcumin: a hybrid sensor toward fluorescence turn-on detection of fluoride ion
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiang
Tan, Xiaoyan
Hu, Yaoping
coredata.dc:description: Trace detection of fluoride ion has gained increasing attention due to fluoride’s close association with biological and environmental processes. Herein, we construct a novel hybrid nanosystem consisting of carbon dots and curcumin for sensitive and selective sensing of F–. Carbon dots are synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of 2,3-diaminopyridine and selenourea in hydrochloric acid. This material is employed as the fluorescent indicator that exhibits intense blue and yellow emission with quantum yields of 12% and 33%, respectively. Curcumin, possessing an absorption peak at 532 nm, can significantly quench the yellow fluorescence of carbon dots through inner filter effect. Curcumin is also used to specifically recognize F–. When F– is added, the curcumin-F– complex generates, which leads to the hypochromatic shift of the absorption band from 532 to 430 nm. In such a case, the inner filter effect reduces, and yellow fluorescence of carbon dots recovers. Thus, a fluorescence turn-on sensor of F– is built based on the carbon dots/curcumin system. The limits of detection and quantitation are measured to be 0.39 and 1.30 μM, respectively. For real usage, the proposed method is applied to determinate F– in tap water and milk samples with relative standard deviations below 7.9%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Curcumin
Hybrid sensor
Fluorescence turn-on detection
Fluoride ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of F–
Monitoring F– Level in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province ( LQ20E020005 ), the State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science ( SKLACLS2004 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City ( 2019A610188 ), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) size distribution, (c) Raman, and (d) FTIR spectra of the as-prepared CDs. Inset in (a): HRTEM image of the as-prepared CDs.
(a) XPS survey, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) Se3d spectra of the as-prepared CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption and (b) PL spectra of the as-prepared CDs. Insets in (a), from left to right: photographs of aqueous CD solution in daylight, under excitation of 340 and 500 nm (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) Absorption spectra of curcumin and curcumin/F–, PL and PLE spectra of CDs. (b) PL spectra of CDs, CDs/curcumin, and CDs/curcumin/F–. (c) Experimental absorption spectra of CDs, curcumin, CDs/curcumin, and the physical addition of the spectra of CDs and curcumin (the theoretical one). (d) PL decay profiles of CDs, CDs/curcumin, and CDs/curcumin/F– (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) PL spectra of the CDs/curcumin solution upon addition of different concentrations of F– (from bottom to top: 0, 2, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, and 100 μM). The excitation wavelength is set at 500 nm. (b) Plot of the fluorescence recovery ratio [(I–I0)/I0] vs F– concentration. I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensities of CDs/curcumin at 562 nm before and after addition of F–, respectively.
The fluorescence recovery ratio [(I–I0)/I0] of the CDs/curcumin solution after addition of F– and interference ions. The concentrations of all ions are 60 μM.

A Schematic of synthesis of blue/yellow emissive CDs and their combination with curcumin for fluorescence turn-on detection of F– (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
The results for F– determination in tap water and milk using the CDs/curcumin sensor and ion chromatography (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Blue/yellow emissive carbon dots coupled with curcumin: a hybrid sensor toward fluorescence turn-on detection of fluoride ion

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing - original draft, Writing - reviewing & editing, Supervision

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Trace detection of fluoride ion has gained increasing attention due to fluoride’s close association with biological and environmental processes. Herein, we construct a novel hybrid nanosystem consisting of carbon dots and curcumin for sensitive and selective sensing of F–. Carbon dots are synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of 2,3-diaminopyridine and selenourea in hydrochloric acid. This material is employed as the fluorescent indicator that exhibits intense blue and yellow emission with quantum yields of 12% and 33%, respectively. Curcumin, possessing an absorption peak at 532 nm, can significantly quench the yellow fluorescence of carbon dots through inner filter effect. Curcumin is also used to specifically recognize F–. When F– is added, the curcumin-F– complex generates, which leads to the hypochromatic shift of the absorption band from 532 to 430 nm. In such a case, the inner filter effect reduces, and yellow fluorescence of carbon dots recovers. Thus, a fluorescence turn-on sensor of F– is built based on the carbon dots/curcumin system. The limits of detection and quantitation are measured to be 0.39 and 1.30 μM, respectively. For real usage, the proposed method is applied to determinate F– in tap water and milk samples with relative standard deviations below 7.9%.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluoride ion (F–) is an important anion that plays vital roles in a wide range of biological, medical, and environmental processes (Zhou et al., 2014). It has a close relationship with dental health and is useful in osteoporosis treatment (Dhiman et al., 2020). However, excessive intake of F– can result in severe gastric and kidney problems (Han et al., 2019a). In some underdeveloped areas, high level of F– in drinking water has caused skeletal fluorosis. Due to its serious negative effects, World Health Organization permits a maximum allowable level of 1 mg L–1 for F– in drinking water (Yang et al., 2017). Hence, it is particularly significant and desirable to develop a facile method for quantitative detection of F–.

In the past several years, various techniques have been employed to determinate F–, including the electrode analysis (Puneet et al., 2016), nuclear magnetic resonance method (Kalita and Murugavel, 2014), colorimetry (Yamaguchi et al., 2001; Bhat et al., 2016), and fluorescence sensing (Yang et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018; Zeng et al., 2020; Nemati et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2018; Nishimura et al., 2013; Halder et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2017; Gao et al., 2019). Different methods have their own merits (Nabid et al., 2012; Sedghi et al., 2015; Behbahani et al., 2015). Among them, the fluorescence approach shares the competitive advantages of good sensitivity, simple manipulation, and high speed. Different fluorescence sensors have been designed for F– detection with different principles. He group synthesized 9,10-azaboraphenanthrene-containing polymers to specially bind with F– for its ratiometric fluorescence sensing (Zhang et al., 2018). Based on unique host-guest interaction, Zeng et al. realized the visual detection of F– using mixed lanthanide metal-organic frameworks (Zeng et al., 2020). Nemati et al. exploited a fluorenone armed calix[4]arene sensor to detect F– through hydrogen bonding (Nemati et al., 2017). The above cases are impressive. However, high cost of the sensors and complicated fabrication procedures limit their wide usage. Therefore, new fluorescent materials with low cost, ease of synthesis, and excellent performance are highly demanded for F– analysis.

Carbon dots (CDs) have ignited extensive interest in both fundamental and applied research due to their fascinating optical properties, low toxicity, facile preparation, easy functionalization, and widely available cheap precursors (Hu et al., 2019; Dhenadhayalan et al., 2020; Chung et al., 2020; Rosso et al., 2020). The most appealing feature of CDs is their photoluminescence (PL). Unfortunately, a majority of CDs emit bright light only at short wavelengths (Jiang et al., 2015; Shamsipur et al., 2017; Shi et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). These CDs commonly display excitation-dependent PL behavior with the strongest peak in the blue or green region. When excitation wavelength increases, the emission wavelength red-shifts, accompanied by a rapid decline of intensity (Sun et al., 2006; Zhu et al., 2015). Currently, fabrication of long-wavelength emissive CDs with intensive yellow or red light is difficult, which restricts their applications in many fields such as sensing, bioimaging, and multicolor patterning.

Herein, we synthesize a novel kind of Se,N-doped CDs that not only possesses a strong blue light but also emits a yellow PL with an unexpected high quantum yield (QY) of 33%. Combined with curcumin, the blue/yellow emissive CDs are utilized to detect F– in a fluorescence turn-on model ( Scheme 1), giving a good detection range and a low detection limit. Based on the proposed sensor, practical F– tests in tap water and milk samples achieve satisfactory results.

Selenourea (98%) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. 2,3-Diaminopyridine (96%) and curcumin (95%) was obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. All other chemicals including acetone and sodium fluoride were analytical grade, and bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

Blue/yellow emissive CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method. Briefly, 60 mg of 2,3-diaminopyridine and 20 mg of selenourea were dispersed in 30 mL of 20 mM HCl solution. The mixture was sonicated for 30 min and then transferred into a 50-mL Teflon autoclave. After heating in an oven at 200 °C for 8 h, the mixture was naturally cooled down to room temperature. A dark red solution containing CDs was obtained. This solution was purified by passing through a 0.22-μm filter membrane and dialyzing against deionized water in a 1000-Da dialysis bag. Finally, the CD powder was collected by freeze-drying.

In a typical procedure, 25 mg of CDs were dispersed in 500 mL of glycine-NaOH buffer (50 mM, pH 9). Then, 2.00 mL of the curcumin acetone solution (12 μM) was mixed with 1.60 mL of the CD solution. Subsequently, 0.40 mL of aqueous NaF solution with a certain concentration was added into the CDs/curcumin mixture. After vigorously shaking to ensure homogenization, the fluorescence spectrum was measured. The excitation wavelength was set at 500 nm, and fluorescence intensity at 562 nm was recorded for quantitative analysis. All the tests were repeated for three times.

Tap water was collected in the laboratory and filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane. Then, different concentrations of NaF were spiked into the tap water. After that, 0.40 mL of the sample, 1.60 mL of the CD solution, and 2.00 mL of the curcumin acetone solution were mixed together for fluorescence detection.

20 mL of milk was mixed with 4 mL of trichloroacetic acid and 4 mL of chloroform. After sonication for 30 min, the mixture was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 5 min to remove the deposit. Then, 1 M NaOH was added into the supernatant to adjust pH to 7. Finally, the sample was filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane and spiked with a certain amount of NaF for detection.

Fig. 1a shows a representative TEM image of the as-prepared CDs, where quasi-spherical nanoparticles are randomly dispersed without aggregation. Their diameters distribute in the range of 1–5 nm, with a mean value of approximately 2.5 nm (Fig. 1b). No clear lattice fringes are found in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset of Fig. 1a), revealing the amorphous structure of CDs. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 1c) displays a D-band at 1388 cm–1 and a G-band at 1593 cm–1, that are related to graphitic and disordered carbons, respectively (Xiong et al., 2020). The stronger intensity of D over G band indicates the presence of many structural defects in CDs which results in a poor graphite crystallinity (Jiang et al., 2018). This result agrees well with the HRTEM observation. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1d) shows predominant characteristic bands at 3433 and 3178 cm–1 for –OH and –NH2 groups, two peaks at 2923 and 2846 cm–1 for C–H stretching vibrations, a band at 1649 cm–1 for C˭O bond, absorptions between 1550 and 1620 cm–1 due to aromatic C˭C/C˭N groups, a sharp peak at 1400 cm–1 ascribed to C–N, and several absorptions in the region of 1000–1250 cm–1 assigned to C–O vibrations. XPS is carried out to further investigate the chemical compositions of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2a, the survey spectrum points out that C, N, O, as well as a tiny amount of Se, are the constituent elements of CDs. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) depicts four carbon types of C–C/C˭C, C–N, C–O, and C˭O/C˭N at 284.4, 285.5, 286.6, and 288.2 eV, respectively (Du et al., 2020). In the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c), three nitrogen species appear including pyridinic N at 398.5 eV, –NH2 bonded to the aromatic clusters at 399.7 eV, and quaternary N at 400.6 eV (Tetsuka et al., 2016), which indicates that a part of N atoms have doped into the carbon framework and the rest exist in the form of amino groups surrounding the CD surface. The Se3d spectrum (Fig. 2d) is fitted into two peaks at 55.7 and 56.6 eV, contributing to Se 3d5/2 and Se 3d3/2, respectively (Kim, Kang (2020); Won et al., 2020). Taken all data together, it is conclude that the as-synthesized material is a kind of Se,N-doped carbonaceous nanoparticles containing amorphous cores and being decorated by abundant functional groups such as –OH and –NH2 on the surface.

The aqueous suspension of CDs is reddish orange in daylight ( Fig. 3a, inset). It has a broad absorption from ultraviolet to visible region (Fig. 3a). The main absorption peak at 315 nm is ascribed to the π–π * transitions of aromatic systems, and a weak shoulder at around 510 nm is attributable to the n–π * transitions of surface groups (Shamsipur et al., 2018). As seen in Fig. 3b, CDs exhibit an untypical excitation-dependent PL behavior with two intensive emission bands at blue and yellow colors. In the PL excitation (PLE) spectra (Fig. S1), it is found that, at 400 nm emission, the excitation peak at 338 nm overlaps well with the absorption peak at 315 nm, indicating that the blue emission is related to the core structures; while at 552 nm emission, the excitation peak at 508 nm is very close to the absorption band at 510 nm, demonstrating that the yellow emission originates from the surface states (Chen et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2021). The decay profiles of the blue and yellow emission are depicted in Fig. S2. Both of them fit a monoexponential formula, giving lifetimes of 4.04 and 5.45 ns, respectively, which further supports that the blue and yellow fluorescence are associated with the core and surface fluorophores, respectively. Using an integrating sphere, CDs dispersed in water possess absolute QYs of 12% and 33% for the blue and yellow emission, respectively. The QY value of 33% is outstanding among the reported yellow emissive CDs (Jiang et al., 2015; Gao et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017; Bao et al., 2015), which is a benefit for advanced optical applications.

To utilize their strong yellow fluorescence, the Se,N-doped CDs are coupled with curcumin to serve as a hybrid sensor for F– detection. As shown in Fig. 4a, the absorption spectrum of curcumin has a broad band centered at 532 nm, substantially overlapping with both the yellow emission and excitation peaks of CDs, which suggests that the yellow fluorescence of CDs can be seriously suppressed by curcumin due to inner filter effect (IFE) (Panigrahi and Mishra, 2019; Chen et al., 2018). After addition of F–, the complex of curcumin-F–generates (Wu et al., 2010), and its absorption band blue-shifts to 430 nm accompanied by the decline of intensity. This change greatly reduces the spectral overlap and disturbs the IFE relationship. Thus, the yellow fluorescence of CDs recovers (Fig. 4b). To further investigate the quenching mechanism, the experimental absorption spectrum of the CDs/curcumin mixture is compared with the theoretical one (the physical addition of the spectra of CDs and curcumin). It is found that the two curves are almost the same (Fig. 4c), indicating the weak interaction between CDs and curcumin and non-formation of new compound (Han et al., 2019b). Therefore, the quenching mechanism of electron transfer between CDs and curcumin is ruled out (Hu and Gao, 2020). Fig. 4d illustrates the PL decay profiles of CDs, CDs/curcumin, and CDs/curcumin/F– with nearly unchanged fluorescence lifetimes of 5.45, 5.39, and 5.31 ns, respectively. This fact implies that IFE, rather than Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), is responsible for the fluorescence quenching, because the fluorescence lifetime keeps at a constant level for IFE, but obviously shortens in the FRET process (Zhang et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of F–
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve a high sensitivity, the detection parameters such as solvent, pH, and mixing ratio of CDs/curcumin are investigated. Curcumin is poorly soluble in water, but easily soluble in acetone. Nevertheless, pure acetone is not favorable for real sample analysis, so a mixed water-acetone solvent in a volume ratio of 1:1 is attempted to use. The pH value has a big impact on the detection system. As shown in Fig. S3, the mixture of CDs/curcumin in water-acetone displays yellow color in the pH range of 3–7. It is orange at pH 8 and 10. Only when pH 9, the solution is pink, and its color changes to yellow after addition of F–. Detailed spectral analysis can be seen in the Supplementary material. This condition meets with the requirements of IFE-based detection. So, pH is fixed at 9. Another typical organic solvent, ethanol, is also mixed with water (volume ratio = 1:1) at different pH (3−11) to serve as a medium for F– detection. But in these cases, no obvious color changes are observed before and after addition of F–. Therefore, ethanol is an unsuitable solvent. The effect of the CDs/curcumin mixing ratio on the fluorescence recovery efficiency is investigated at a fixed F– concentration. As seen in Fig. S4, 6 μM of curcumin and 20 mg L–1 of CDs are selected as the optimum mixing ratio.

Under the optimal conditions, various concentrations of F– are added into the CDs/curcumin solution. As recorded in Fig. 5a, the yellow fluorescence of CDs gradually recovers with the F– concentration increasing from 0 to 100 μM. A nice linear relationship (R2 = 0.998) is observed between the intensity recovery ratio [(I–I0)/I0] at 562 nm and F– concentration in the range of 2–60 μM (Fig. 5b). The fitted equation can be expressed as (I–I0)/I0 = 0.0678 × F– (μM) – 0.0302. The detection limit is calculated to be 0.39 μM according to the rule of 3σ/k, much lower than the maximum level (1 mg L–1, equivalent to 52.6 μM) of F– in drinking water permitted by World Health Organization (Yang et al., 2017). The limit of quantitation is 1.30 μM. The selectivity of the CDs/curcumin sensor towards F– is evaluated by adding a series of common ions at the same conditions. Various cations and anions, including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cl–, Br–, I–, CN–, CH3COO–, NO2 –, NO3 –, ClO–, ClO4 –, SO4 2–, CO3 2–, Cr2O7 2–, and PO4 3–, result in negligible enhancement of the yellow fluorescence of the CDs/curcumin sensor ( Fig. 6), confirming the excellent selectivity. Compared with the formerly reported fluorescence methods for F– detection (Table S1) (Yang et al., 2017; Zeng et al., 2020; Nemati et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2018; Nishimura et al., 2013; Halder et al., 2019), our sensing system is sensitive, selective, and economic, benefiting from the well-designed fluorescence turn-on model and high QY, low cost of the materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring F– Level in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To test its practicability, the CDs/curcumin sensor is applied for monitoring F– in tap water and milk. After pretreatments as described in the experimental section, both samples are spiked with standard solutions of F–. As listed in Table 1, the relative recovery (RR) ranges from 92.6% to 109.5%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is lower than 7.9%. These results are satisfying and quite consistent with those obtained from ion chromatographic method, suggesting the high accuracy and reliability of the proposed method for quantitative determination of F– in real samples.

In summary, we have prepared Se,N-doped CDs emitting blue and yellow lights with high QYs of 12% and 33%. Due to efficient IFE, curcumin can remarkably quench the yellow fluorescence of CDs. After addition of F–, the complex of curcumin-F– generates, which results in IFE weakening and fluorescence recovery. Thus, a hybrid sensor based on CDs/curcumin is built for fluorescence turn-on detection of F–. This sensor exhibits low detection limit (0.39 μM) and high selectivity, enabling reliable assay of F– in tap water and milk samples. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that a nanosystem constructed by CDs and a dye molecule has been applied as a promising sensor for F– determination. Our work not only provides a new strategy for fabricating long-wavelength emissive CDs, but also expands their applications in environmental monitoring and food safety.

Xiang Zhang: Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Software. Xiaoyan Tan: Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Software. Yaoping Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing - original draft preparation, Writing - review and editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LQ20E020005), the State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2004), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City (2019A610188), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.125184.
